perturbation.original_text: 1. "INFORMATION" shall mean any information, technical data or know-how relating to the business, services or products of the disclosing party or a third party, including without limitation any research, products, services, developments, inventions, processes, techniques, designs, components, parts, documents, drawings, electronic files, data sketches, plans, programs, specifications, software, and/or distribution, engineering, marketing, financial, merchandising, sales, and salary information and/or other materials (hereinafter collectively referred to as "INFORMATION") which is disclosed by such party or on its behalf, before or after the date hereof, to the other party or its employees or agents, directly or indirectly, in writing, orally, electronically, or by drawings or inspection.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. "INFORMATION" shall mean any information, technical data or know-how relating to the business, services or products of the disclosing party or a third party, including without limitation any research, products, services, developments, inventions, processes, techniques, designs, components, parts, documents, drawings, electronic files, data sketches, plans, programs, specifications, software, and/or distribution, engineering, marketing, financial, merchandising, sales, and salary information and/or other materials (hereinafter collectively referred to as "Confidential Materials") which is disclosed by such party or on its behalf, before or after the date hereof, to the other party or its employees or agents, directly or indirectly, in writing, orally, electronically, or by drawings or inspection.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'INFORMATION' is redefined mid-sentence as 'Confidential Materials.' This creates confusion because later sections refer to 'INFORMATION,' creating ambiguity as to whether 'Confidential Materials' is included. Specifically, Section 4 refers to 'INFORMATION' and 'all manifestations thereof,' creating uncertainty if this includes the redefined 'Confidential Materials.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. The receiving party shall, upon request of the disclosing party: (i) return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all INFORMATION and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certify to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been returned.
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'INFORMATION' as 'Confidential Materials' within the definition of 'INFORMATION' itself. This creates a direct conflict with Section 4, which refers to 'INFORMATION' and all manifestations thereof. Because the original 'INFORMATION' definition no longer exists as written and has been changed mid-definition to 'Confidential Materials', it is unclear whether Section 4 applies to what was originally included in 'INFORMATION', thus creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. The receiving party shall hold in trust and confidence, and not disclose to others, by any means, any and all INFORMATION disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. The receiving party shall hold in trust and confidence, and not disclose to others, by any means, any and all Data disclosed under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The text now uses 'Data' instead of 'INFORMATION,' but doesn't specify if 'Data' and 'INFORMATION' are interchangeable. Because the term 'INFORMATION' is defined earlier and 'Data' is used here without definition, it is ambiguous if all references to 'INFORMATION' also imply 'Data.' This is especially problematic in Section 5, which discusses the expiration of duties related to 'INFORMATION.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. The receiving party's duties under Section 2 of this Agreement expire with respect to any particular item of INFORMATION five years after the date of disclosure hereunder to the receiving party, or three years after the expiration or termination of the relationship between the parties to this Agreement, whichever is later.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'INFORMATION' to 'Data' in Section 2 introduces ambiguity because 'INFORMATION' is a defined term, while 'Data' is not. Section 5 refers specifically to 'INFORMATION,' and it's now unclear whether the obligations in Section 5 also apply to 'Data' disclosed under the modified Section 2. This creates a direct conflict and uncertainty regarding the expiration of duties related to the disclosed data, fitting the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction.'

perturbation.original_text: 3. Title to all property received by the receiving party from the disclosing party, including all INFORMATION, shall remain at all times the sole property of the disclosing party, and this Agreement shall not be construed to grant to receiving party any licenses or similar rights to such property or INFORMATION (including all intellectual property) disclosed to the receiving party hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Title to all property received by the receiving party from the disclosing party, including all intelligence, shall remain at all times the sole property of the disclosing party, and this Agreement shall not be construed to grant to receiving party any licenses or similar rights to such property or INFORMATION (including all intellectual property) disclosed to the receiving party hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'INFORMATION' with 'intelligence' in the phrase 'including all INFORMATION' suggests that 'intelligence' is a specific type of property, while 'INFORMATION' is used in the rest of the section, making it ambiguous whether the intellectual property protections extend to 'intelligence' as well. This creates uncertainty when referencing Intellectual Property Claims
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Title to all property received by the receiving party from the disclosing party, including all intelligence, shall remain at all times the sole property of the disclosing party, and this Agreement shall not be construed to grant to receiving party any licenses or similar rights to such property or INFORMATION (including all intellectual property) disclosed to the receiving party hereunder. Nothing in this Agreement shall limit or restrict the rights of the disclosing party to assert infringement or other intellectual property claims against the receiving party or to impose on either party any obligation to disclose any INFORMATION or to purchase or sell any products.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'INFORMATION' to 'intelligence' introduces ambiguity regarding whether intellectual property protections extend to 'intelligence' as well. The Contradicted Text includes the changed text, highlighting that the agreement protects intelligence. However, the modification raises uncertainty because while the contradicted section uses the changed text and references intelligence, it also still refers to 'INFORMATION (including all intellectual property)'. The change makes it ambiguous whether the intellectual property protections explicitly apply to intelligence, creating a contradiction related to intellectual property claims. Because intelligence is now a separate term and is used in conjunction with 'INFORMATION (including all intellectual property)', this creates a scenario where the terms are misaligned and introduces a strong in-text contradiction, according to the definition provided.

perturbation.original_text: 4. The receiving party shall, upon request of the disclosing party: (i) return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all INFORMATION and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certify to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The receiving party shall, upon request of the disclosing party: (i) return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all intelligence and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certify to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been returned.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'INFORMATION' to 'intelligence' in the first part of the sentence but leaving the second instance as 'INFORMATION,' the text introduces an internal contradiction. It is unclear if the certification in part (ii) only applies to the original 'INFORMATION' or also includes the 'intelligence' mentioned in part (i). This ambiguity affects enforcement because it's uncertain what the receiving party must certify.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. The receiving party shall, upon request of the disclosing party: (i) return to the disclosing party all documents, drawings, equipment and other tangible materials, including all intelligence and all manifestations thereof, delivered to the receiving party under this Agreement, and all copies and reproductions thereof; and (ii) certify to the disclosing party that all such INFORMATION has been returned.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'INFORMATION' to 'intelligence' in the changed text while keeping 'INFORMATION' in the second part of the same sentence introduces a direct contradiction. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, which means the contradiction is present within the same clause. This creates ambiguity and uncertainty about what the receiving party must certify, fulfilling the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: (b) Receiving party shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration Laws and Regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the disclosure or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed countries.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Receiving party shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration Laws and Guidelines and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the disclosure or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed countries.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Regulations' to 'Guidelines' in Section 6(b) introduces a significant difference in enforceability. 'Regulations' have the force of law, while 'Guidelines' are advisory. This weakens the obligation to comply with export controls, creating an in-text contradiction about the binding nature of export compliance. The term 'Regulations' is used later in Section 6(b) when discussing the action being restricted.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. The parties further agree to the following terms and conditions: (b) Receiving party shall adhere to the U.S. Export Administration Laws and Guidelines and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products received from the disclosure or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed countries.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Regulations' to 'Guidelines' in Section 6(b) alters the binding nature of the export control requirement. 'Regulations' are legally binding, whereas 'Guidelines' are advisory. The contradicted text is identical to the changed text, and the explanation states that this change creates an in-text contradiction. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists because the modification introduces ambiguity regarding the enforceability of the export compliance obligation.
